Numerical Linear Algebra

Working with the SVD: Low-Rank Approximation, Least Squares, and the Pseudoinverse

Working with the SVD: Low-Rank Approximation, Least Squares, and the Pseudoinverse

The previous chapter introduced the singular value decomposition and used it to explain matrix norms, conditioning, and the four fundamental subspaces. This chapter puts the SVD to work. We’ll see that the factorization A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT can be repackaged as a sum of simple rank-one pieces, that truncating this sum gives the provably best possible low-rank approximation of AAA, and that the whole machinery combines into a single formula — the Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse — that solves every least-squares problem this course has considered, even the ones with no unique solution.

We start by restating the definition precisely, since the exact shapes of UUU, Σ\SigmaΣ, VVV matter for the algebra that follows.

Singular Value Decomposition

Let A∈Rm×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n. Its singular value decomposition is A=UΣVT,A = U\Sigma V^T,A=UΣVT, where U∈Rm×mU\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m}U∈Rm×m and V∈Rn×nV\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}V∈Rn×n are orthogonal (UTU=ImU^TU = I_mUTU=Im​, VTV=InV^TV=I_nVTV=In​), and Σ∈Rm×n\Sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}Σ∈Rm×n is rectangular-diagonal:

Σ=[σ10σ2⋱0σp],p=min⁡(m,n),\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix}\sigma_1 &&& 0\\ &\sigma_2&&\\ &&\ddots&\\ 0&&&\sigma_p\end{bmatrix}, \qquad p=\min(m,n),Σ=​σ1​0​σ2​​⋱​0σp​​​,p=min(m,n),

with σ1≥σ2≥⋯≥σp≥0\sigma_1\ge\sigma_2\ge\cdots\ge\sigma_p\ge0σ1​≥σ2​≥⋯≥σp​≥0. The columns of UUU are the left singular vectors, the columns of VVV are the right singular vectors, and the nonzero diagonal entries of Σ\SigmaΣ are the singular values.

If rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A) = rrank(A)=r, only the first rrr singular values are nonzero, so most of UUU and VVV is doing nothing in the product. Trimming away that dead weight gives a smaller, equally exact factorization:

Compact SVD

A=UrΣrVrT,A = U_r\Sigma_rV_r^T,A=Ur​Σr​VrT​, where Ur∈Rm×rU_r\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times r}Ur​∈Rm×r holds u1,…,uru_1,\dots,u_ru1​,…,ur​, Vr∈Rn×rV_r\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times r}Vr​∈Rn×r holds v1,…,vrv_1,\dots,v_rv1​,…,vr​, and Σr=diag⁡(σ1,…,σr)\Sigma_r = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_r)Σr​=diag(σ1​,…,σr​). This is the most useful form in computation, since it discards exactly the parts of U,Σ,VU,\Sigma,VU,Σ,V that contribute nothing to AAA — the same “thin” philosophy behind the reduced QR factorization from earlier in the course.

SVD as a Sum of Rank-One Matrices

Matrix multiplication has an alternative reading that’s easy to overlook: UrΣrVrTU_r\Sigma_rV_r^TUr​Σr​VrT​ can be expanded column-by-column rather than treated as a single block product.

Outer-product expansion

From the compact SVD, A=∑j=1rσj ujvjT.A = \sum_{j=1}^r \sigma_j\,u_jv_j^T.A=∑j=1r​σj​uj​vjT​. Each term ujvjTu_jv_j^Tuj​vjT​ is an outer product — a column times a row — and every outer product has rank exactly 111 (when σj≠0\sigma_j\neq0σj​=0). So the SVD writes any matrix, no matter how complicated, as a weighted sum of the simplest possible building blocks: rank-one matrices.

Because the singular values are sorted in decreasing order, this sum is organized from “most important” to “least important” — σ1u1v1T\sigma_1u_1v_1^Tσ1​u1​v1T​ captures the single largest rank-one piece of AAA, σ2u2v2T\sigma_2u_2v_2^Tσ2​u2​v2T​ the next largest, and so on. That ordering is what makes truncation meaningful.

Truncated SVD

For 0≤ν≤r0\le\nu\le r0≤ν≤r, define Aν=∑j=1νσjujvjT.A_\nu = \sum_{j=1}^\nu \sigma_ju_jv_j^T.Aν​=∑j=1ν​σj​uj​vjT​. AνA_\nuAν​ has rank at most ν\nuν, and it keeps only the ν\nuν largest singular values together with their singular vectors. This is the standard way to build a low-rank approximation of AAA — and, as we’ll see shortly, it isn’t just a good approximation, it’s the best possible one.

Singular Values, Rank, and the Fundamental Subspaces

The rank-one expansion also makes it easy to see exactly how AAA and ATA^TAT act on the singular vectors themselves.

Action of AAA and ATA^TAT on singular vectors

If A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT with rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A)=rrank(A)=r, then for 1≤j≤r1\le j\le r1≤j≤r, Avj=σjuj,ATuj=σjvj,Av_j = \sigma_ju_j, \qquad A^Tu_j = \sigma_jv_j,Avj​=σj​uj​,ATuj​=σj​vj​, while for j>rj > rj>r, Avj=0,ATuj=0.Av_j = 0, \qquad A^Tu_j = 0.Avj​=0,ATuj​=0.

Proof

Since VTvj=ejV^Tv_j = e_jVTvj​=ej​ (the jjj-th standard basis vector), Avj=UΣVTvj=UΣejAv_j = U\Sigma V^Tv_j = U\Sigma e_jAvj​=UΣVTvj​=UΣej​. If j≤rj\le rj≤r, then Σej=σjej\Sigma e_j = \sigma_je_jΣej​=σj​ej​, giving Avj=σjUej=σjujAv_j = \sigma_jUe_j = \sigma_ju_jAvj​=σj​Uej​=σj​uj​. If j>rj>rj>r, the jjj-th diagonal entry of Σ\SigmaΣ is zero, so Σej=0\Sigma e_j = 0Σej​=0 and Avj=0Av_j=0Avj​=0. The argument for ATujA^Tu_jATuj​ is identical, using UTuj=ejU^Tu_j = e_jUTuj​=ej​.

This lemma has two immediate consequences that echo results from the previous chapter — reproduced here because they follow so directly from the algebra above that it’s worth seeing them fall out cleanly.

Rank equals the number of nonzero singular values

rank⁡(A)=#{j:σj≠0}.\operatorname{rank}(A) = \#\{j : \sigma_j \neq 0\}.rank(A)=#{j:σj​=0}. This is immediate: Σ\SigmaΣ has exactly that many nonzero diagonal entries, and rank is preserved under multiplication by the invertible (in fact orthogonal) matrices UUU and VVV.

Fundamental subspaces from the SVD

With rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A)=rrank(A)=r: Range⁡(A)=span⁡{u1,…,ur},Range⁡(AT)=span⁡{v1,…,vr},\operatorname{Range}(A) = \operatorname{span}\{u_1,\dots,u_r\}, \qquad \operatorname{Range}(A^T) = \operatorname{span}\{v_1,\dots,v_r\},Range(A)=span{u1​,…,ur​},Range(AT)=span{v1​,…,vr​}, Null⁡(A)=span⁡{vr+1,…,vn},Null⁡(AT)=span⁡{ur+1,…,um}.\operatorname{Null}(A) = \operatorname{span}\{v_{r+1},\dots,v_n\}, \qquad \operatorname{Null}(A^T) = \operatorname{span}\{u_{r+1},\dots,u_m\}.Null(A)=span{vr+1​,…,vn​},Null(AT)=span{ur+1​,…,um​}. The columns of UUU and VVV together give orthonormal bases for all four fundamental subspaces at once.

Why this holds: for j≤rj\le rj≤r, Avj=σjujAv_j=\sigma_ju_jAvj​=σj​uj​ places each uju_juj​ inside Range⁡(A)\operatorname{Range}(A)Range(A); since u1,…,uru_1,\dots,u_ru1​,…,ur​ are orthonormal and there are exactly rrr of them, they form a basis for it. For j>rj>rj>r, Avj=0Av_j=0Avj​=0 places those vjv_jvj​ in Null⁡(A)\operatorname{Null}(A)Null(A). The statements for ATA^TAT follow by the same argument applied to the transpose relation.

Best Low-Rank Approximation

We claimed earlier that truncating the rank-one sum gives the best rank-ν\nuν approximation of AAA. This is a genuine theorem, not just a reasonable-sounding heuristic, and it’s one of the most consequential results built on the SVD.

Eckart–Young Theorem

Let A∈Rm×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n have singular values σ1≥⋯≥σr>0\sigma_1\ge\cdots\ge\sigma_r>0σ1​≥⋯≥σr​>0, and let Aν=∑j=1νσjujvjTA_\nu = \sum_{j=1}^\nu\sigma_ju_jv_j^TAν​=∑j=1ν​σj​uj​vjT​ for 0≤ν≤r0\le\nu\le r0≤ν≤r. Then AνA_\nuAν​ minimizes the distance to AAA over every matrix of rank at most ν\nuν: ∥A−Aν∥2=inf⁡rank⁡(B)≤ν∥A−B∥2=σν+1\|A-A_\nu\|_2 = \inf_{\operatorname{rank}(B)\le\nu}\|A-B\|_2 = \sigma_{\nu+1}∥A−Aν​∥2​=infrank(B)≤ν​∥A−B∥2​=σν+1​ (with σr+1:=0\sigma_{r+1}:=0σr+1​:=0 by convention). The same optimality holds in Frobenius norm: ∥A−Aν∥F2=∑j=ν+1rσj2.\|A-A_\nu\|_F^2 = \sum_{j=\nu+1}^r\sigma_j^2.∥A−Aν​∥F2​=∑j=ν+1r​σj2​.

In other words: among all possible rank-ν\nuν matrices — not just other truncations of AAA‘s own SVD — none can get closer to AAA than AνA_\nuAν​ does. Discarding the smallest singular values first is not merely convenient; it is provably optimal.

Interpretation

Since the terms σjujvjT\sigma_ju_jv_j^Tσj​uj​vjT​ are sorted by size, truncated SVD keeps the most significant structure of AAA first and discards the least significant last — AνA_\nuAν​ is the optimal way to compress AAA down to rank ν\nuν. This is the mathematical foundation for PCA, image and data compression, and denoising.

The Eckart–Young theorem also has a robustness consequence: it tells you how much a matrix can be perturbed before its rank is forced to drop.

Full-rank stability

Suppose AAA has rank rrr with smallest nonzero singular value σr>0\sigma_r>0σr​>0. If ∥A−B∥2<σr\|A-B\|_2 < \sigma_r∥A−B∥2​<σr​, then rank⁡(B)≥r\operatorname{rank}(B)\ge rrank(B)≥r. In particular, if AAA has full rank, so does any BBB close enough to it.

Proof

If rank⁡(B)≤r−1\operatorname{rank}(B)\le r-1rank(B)≤r−1, Eckart–Young says ∥A−B∥2≥σr\|A-B\|_2 \ge \sigma_r∥A−B∥2​≥σr​ — since BBB would then be a rank-(r−1)(r-1)(r−1) matrix, and no such matrix can beat the optimal rank-(r−1)(r-1)(r−1) approximation’s error of σr\sigma_rσr​. This contradicts ∥A−B∥2<σr\|A-B\|_2 <\sigma_r∥A−B∥2​<σr​, so rank⁡(B)≥r\operatorname{rank}(B)\ge rrank(B)≥r.

Numerical Rank

In exact arithmetic, rank is unambiguous: it’s the count of nonzero singular values. But recall from the numerical-stability chapter that floating-point computation never produces exact zeros — rounding error tends to leave tiny nonzero residues where a true zero should be. This means computed singular values that are “supposed to” be zero often show up instead as extremely small but nonzero numbers, which would make a naive rank calculation wildly overcount.

Numerical rank

The numerical rank of AAA is the number of singular values significantly larger than a chosen computational tolerance. A common rule: find kkk such that σk>tol,σk+1≤tol,\sigma_k > \text{tol}, \qquad \sigma_{k+1}\le\text{tol},σk​>tol,σk+1​≤tol, with the tolerance scaled to machine precision, typically tol≈max⁡(m,n) σ1 εmach.\text{tol}\approx \max(m,n)\,\sigma_1\,\varepsilon_{\text{mach}}.tol≈max(m,n)σ1​εmach​.

This is the same swamping concern from the floating-point chapter, now viewed through the lens of singular values: a “numerically zero” singular value is one that’s small enough to be indistinguishable from rounding noise relative to the largest singular value σ1\sigma_1σ1​.

The spectral norm, revisited

Recall from the previous chapter that σ1=∥A∥2\sigma_1 = \|A\|_2σ1​=∥A∥2​. This is worth restating here because it’s exactly the reference scale used in the tolerance formula above — the numerical-rank cutoff is always measured relative to the matrix’s own overall size.

SVD vs. Eigenvalue Decomposition

It’s worth directly contrasting the SVD with the eigenvalue decomposition (EVD) from two chapters ago, now that both are fully developed.

Recall: if a square A∈Rn×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}A∈Rn×n has nnn linearly independent eigenvectors, it diagonalizes as A=XΛX−1A = X\Lambda X^{-1}A=XΛX−1 — but not every square matrix qualifies, so EVD in this form does not always exist.

FeatureSVDEVD
Applies toEvery matrix A∈Rm×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×nSquare matrices only
ExistenceAlways existsRequires diagonalizability
Bases usedTwo orthogonal bases, UUU and VVVOne basis, XXX
OrthogonalityAlways orthogonalOnly in special cases (e.g. symmetric matrices)
Diagonal valuesNonnegative singular valuesEigenvalues, possibly negative or complex

The two are not unrelated, though — the SVD is built directly from the EVD of a matrix that’s always guaranteed to be well-behaved.

Singular values from ATAA^TAATA

The nonzero singular values of AAA are the square roots of the nonzero eigenvalues of ATAA^TAATA (equivalently, of AATAA^TAAT).

Proof

Starting from A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT: ATA=(UΣVT)T(UΣVT)=VΣTUTUΣVT=V(ΣTΣ)VT.A^TA = (U\Sigma V^T)^T(U\Sigma V^T) = V\Sigma^TU^TU\Sigma V^T = V(\Sigma^T\Sigma)V^T.ATA=(UΣVT)T(UΣVT)=VΣTUTUΣVT=V(ΣTΣ)VT. Since VVV is orthogonal, this is literally an eigenvalue decomposition of ATAA^TAATA, with eigenvalues equal to the diagonal entries of ΣTΣ\Sigma^T\SigmaΣTΣ — namely σ12,σ22,…\sigma_1^2,\sigma_2^2,\dotsσ12​,σ22​,…. So the singular values are the square roots of those eigenvalues.

Practical consequence

ATAA^TAATA and AATAA^TAAT share the same nonzero eigenvalues, so singular values can be computed from either one — in practice, whichever is smaller. This is the same fact used two chapters ago to explain where SVD’s singular values “come from” in the first place; here it becomes a concrete computational shortcut.

Least Squares via the SVD

We now arrive at the payoff that ties this entire unit together. Recall the least-squares problem from several chapters back: given A∈Rm×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n and b∈Rmb\in\mathbb{R}^mb∈Rm, find min⁡x∈Rn∥b−Ax∥2.\min_{x\in\mathbb{R}^n}\|b-Ax\|_2.minx∈Rn​∥b−Ax∥2​. If AAA doesn’t have full column rank, this minimizer isn’t unique — infinitely many xxx can achieve the same minimal residual. In that case we additionally want the smallest such xxx: min⁡∥x∥2\min\|x\|_2min∥x∥2​ among all minimizers. QR factorization (from the numerical-stability chapters) handled the full-rank case cleanly; the SVD handles every case, rank-deficient or not.

Step 1 — insert the SVD. Let A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT with rank⁡(A)=r\operatorname{rank}(A)=rrank(A)=r. Because multiplying by the orthogonal matrix UTU^TUT never changes a vector’s length (the same fact used throughout this course, from reflectors onward): ∥b−Ax∥2=∥UT(b−Ax)∥2=∥UTb−Σ(VTx)∥2.\|b-Ax\|_2 = \|U^T(b-Ax)\|_2 = \|U^Tb - \Sigma(V^Tx)\|_2.∥b−Ax∥2​=∥UT(b−Ax)∥2​=∥UTb−Σ(VTx)∥2​. Define c=UTbc = U^Tbc=UTb and y=VTxy=V^Txy=VTx; the problem becomes min⁡y∥c−Σy∥2\min_y\|c-\Sigma y\|_2miny​∥c−Σy∥2​ — the same problem, just viewed in the coordinate system where AAA‘s action is pure diagonal scaling.

Step 2 — split by rank. Partition c=[c^d]c = \begin{bmatrix}\hat c\\d\end{bmatrix}c=[c^d​], y=[y^z]y=\begin{bmatrix}\hat y\\z\end{bmatrix}y=[y^​z​], and Σ=[Σ^000]\Sigma = \begin{bmatrix}\hat\Sigma&0\\0&0\end{bmatrix}Σ=[Σ^0​00​] where Σ^=diag⁡(σ1,…,σr)\hat\Sigma = \operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_r)Σ^=diag(σ1​,…,σr​). Then ∥c−Σy∥22=∑i=1r∣c^i−σiy^i∣2+∑i=r+1m∣di∣2.\|c-\Sigma y\|_2^2 = \sum_{i=1}^r|\hat c_i - \sigma_i\hat y_i|^2 + \sum_{i=r+1}^m|d_i|^2.∥c−Σy∥22​=∑i=1r​∣c^i​−σi​y^​i​∣2+∑i=r+1m​∣di​∣2.

Step 3 — minimize. The second sum doesn’t involve yyy at all — it’s an unavoidable leftover residual. The first sum is minimized exactly, term by term, by choosing y^i=c^i/σi\hat y_i = \hat c_i/\sigma_iy^​i​=c^i​/σi​ for i=1,…,ri=1,\dots,ri=1,…,r. The remaining components zzz of yyy multiply only the zero block of Σ\SigmaΣ, so they have zero effect on the residual — they’re completely free.

Step 4 — break the tie with minimum norm. Since VVV is orthogonal, ∥x∥2=∥y∥2\|x\|_2 = \|y\|_2∥x∥2​=∥y∥2​, so minimizing ∥x∥2\|x\|_2∥x∥2​ is the same as minimizing ∥y∥2\|y\|_2∥y∥2​. Since the free components zzz don’t affect the residual, the smallest-norm choice is simply z=0z=0z=0.

Algorithm: Least squares via SVD
  1. Compute A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT.
  2. Compute c=UTbc = U^Tbc=UTb.
  3. For i=1,…,ri=1,\dots,ri=1,…,r, set yi=ci/σiy_i = c_i/\sigma_iyi​=ci​/σi​.
  4. For i=r+1,…,ni=r+1,\dots,ni=r+1,…,n, set yi=0y_i = 0yi​=0.
  5. Recover x=Vyx = Vyx=Vy.

Notice that this algorithm degrades gracefully exactly where the normal equations and even plain QR can fail: if AAA doesn’t have full column rank, ATAA^TAATA isn’t invertible and the normal equations break down entirely — but the SVD approach simply sets yi=0y_i=0yi​=0 for the deficient directions and moves on.

The Moore–Penrose Pseudoinverse

The four-step algorithm above can be packaged into a single matrix, giving a direct generalization of A−1A^{-1}A−1 that works even when AAA isn’t square or invertible.

Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse

If A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT has rank rrr, its pseudoinverse is A†=VΣ†UT,A^\dagger = V\Sigma^\dagger U^T,A†=VΣ†UT, where Σ†∈Rn×m\Sigma^\dagger\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times m}Σ†∈Rn×m replaces each nonzero singular value σi\sigma_iσi​ with 1/σi1/\sigma_i1/σi​: Σ†=[Σ^−1000].\Sigma^\dagger = \begin{bmatrix}\hat\Sigma^{-1}&0\\0&0\end{bmatrix}.Σ†=[Σ^−10​00​]. Equivalently, in rank-one form: A†=∑i=1r1σiviuiT.A^\dagger = \sum_{i=1}^r \frac{1}{\sigma_i}v_iu_i^T.A†=∑i=1r​σi​1​vi​uiT​.

Minimum-norm least-squares solution

For any A∈Rm×nA\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A∈Rm×n and b∈Rmb\in\mathbb{R}^mb∈Rm, the unique vector that both minimizes ∥b−Ax∥2\|b-Ax\|_2∥b−Ax∥2​ and, among all such minimizers, has smallest norm ∥x∥2\|x\|_2∥x∥2​, is x=A†b.x = A^\dagger b.x=A†b.

Proof

From the derivation above, the least-squares minimizer in rotated coordinates is y=Σ†cy = \Sigma^\dagger cy=Σ†c with c=UTbc=U^Tbc=UTb. Since x=Vyx=Vyx=Vy, x=VΣ†c=VΣ†UTb=A†b.x = V\Sigma^\dagger c = V\Sigma^\dagger U^Tb = A^\dagger b.x=VΣ†c=VΣ†UTb=A†b.

What the pseudoinverse generalizes
  • If AAA is square and invertible, A†=A−1A^\dagger = A^{-1}A†=A−1 — the pseudoinverse reduces to the ordinary inverse exactly when one exists.
  • If Ax=bAx=bAx=b is inconsistent (the overdetermined case from the least-squares chapters), A†bA^\dagger bA†b gives the best least-squares fit.
  • If Ax=bAx=bAx=b has infinitely many solutions (the rank-deficient case), A†bA^\dagger bA†b selects the one of smallest Euclidean norm.

This closes the arc that began several chapters ago with the normal equations: what started as “solve ATAx^=ATbA^TA\hat x = A^TbATAx^=ATb, but watch out — it squares the condition number” has, by way of QR and now the SVD, become a single well-behaved formula, x=A†bx = A^\dagger bx=A†b, that works for every matrix regardless of shape or rank.

Key Takeaways

Main facts to remember
  • Every matrix has an SVD, A=UΣVTA = U\Sigma V^TA=UΣVT, with no restrictions on shape, symmetry, or diagonalizability.
  • rank⁡(A)\operatorname{rank}(A)rank(A) equals the number of nonzero singular values, and the singular vectors give orthonormal bases for all four fundamental subspaces.
  • The truncated SVD Aν=∑j=1νσjujvjTA_\nu = \sum_{j=1}^\nu \sigma_ju_jv_j^TAν​=∑j=1ν​σj​uj​vjT​ is the provably best rank-ν\nuν approximation to AAA, in both spectral and Frobenius norm (Eckart–Young).
  • Singular values are the square roots of the eigenvalues of ATAA^TAATA — the SVD is, structurally, the EVD of a matrix that’s always symmetric, applied to any matrix at all.
  • The minimum-norm least-squares solution to Ax≈bAx\approx bAx≈b, for any AAA, is x=A†bx = A^\dagger bx=A†b — the single formula that generalizes everything this course has built toward solving Ax=bAx=bAx=b approximately.
← Previous The Singular Value Decomposition Next → Eigenvalues, Eigenvectors, and the Spectral Theorem
